cards.short: A subject plus a verb, expressing a complete thought.
cards.detail:
A sentence is the smallest complete move a writer can make: someone or something (the subject) doing or being something (the verb), forming a thought that stands on its own.

Ice melts. · The crowd roared. · She is a pilot.
Everything else you will ever learn about sentences — objects, phrases, clauses — is an expansion of this kernel.

The Kernel Test
Point to the subject. Point to the verb. Ask: does this feel finished? If yes, you have a sentence.

cards.short: The WHO or WHAT the sentence is about.
cards.detail:
The subject is the noun or pronoun that performs (or is linked to) the action. Every complete sentence needs one.

How to find it: ask "Who?" or "What?" before the verb.
Complete vs. simple: "The tall coach" is the complete subject; "coach" alone is the simple subject.

Quick Test
The subject usually comes before the verb — except in questions ("Did she leave?") and inverted sentences ("Here comes the bus").

cards.short: Everything said about the subject — always contains the verb.
cards.detail:
The predicate is everything that is NOT the subject. It says what the subject does, is, or has.

Simple predicate = just the verb. Complete predicate = the verb plus all its objects, complements, and modifiers.

Key Idea
Subject + predicate = a complete sentence. Leave either out and you have a fragment — an unfinished move.

cards.short: The action or state — the engine of every predicate.
cards.detail:
Action verbs show doing (run, give, write).
Linking verbs connect the subject to a description (is, seem, become, look, taste).

The verb type determines what follows: action verbs can take objects; linking verbs take complements.

Linking Verb Test
Substitute "is/are." If the sentence still makes sense, it's likely a linking verb. "The soup tastes great" → "The soup is great." ✓

cards.short: A group of words missing its subject, its verb, or its finished thought.
cards.detail:
A fragment is an incomplete move — a sentence that hasn't finished happening yet. Something is missing: the subject, the verb, or the sense of completion.

Running down the hall. (who ran?)
The girl in the red jacket. (what did she do?)
Because the power went out. (…then what happened?)
Writers use deliberate fragments for punch — Not today. — but a fragment you didn't choose leaves the reader hanging.

The Fix Is a Build
Don't just spot fragments — finish them. Add the missing subject or verb, or attach the fragment to the sentence it belongs to.

cards.short: The person or thing that receives the action.
cards.detail:
Ask "whom?" or "what?" after an action verb. Not every sentence has one — intransitive verbs (sleep, arrive) don't take objects.

Quick Test
Make it passive. If you can, the DO becomes the subject: "She kicked the ball" → "The ball was kicked."

cards.short: Who receives the direct object — the secondary receiver.
cards.detail:
The IO tells TO WHOM or FOR WHOM the action is done. It only exists alongside a DO.

Two positions:
Before the DO: "She gave him a book."
As a prepositional phrase instead: "She gave a book to him."

Quick Test
Rephrase with "to ___" or "for ___." If the meaning holds, it's an IO. Note that only some verbs accept one: you can hand someone a note, but you explain a lesson to someone.

cards.short: Words that complete the verb's meaning by describing or renaming.
cards.detail:
Complements "complete" the verb. Two families:

Subject complements follow linking verbs → describe or rename the subject.
— Predicate nominative (noun): She is a doctor.
— Predicate adjective (adjective): She seems happy.

Object complements follow a DO → describe or rename it.
— They elected her president. (noun)
— The news made him anxious. (adjective)

Complement vs. DO
A complement = the subject or object. "She is a doctor" → she = doctor. A DO is a separate thing: "She visited a doctor" → she ≠ doctor.

cards.short: A noun that renames the subject through a linking verb.
cards.detail:
A predicate nominative (PN) is a noun or noun phrase after a linking verb that renames the subject. Subject = PN.

She is a doctor. She = a doctor.
My favorite food is pizza.
PN vs. DO
"She is a doctor" (PN — linking verb, she = doctor).
"She visited a doctor" (DO — action verb, she ≠ doctor).

cards.short: An adjective that describes the subject through a linking verb.
cards.detail:
A predicate adjective (PA) appears after a linking verb and uses an adjective to describe the subject.

The soup tastes delicious.
She seems tired.
PA vs. PN
If the complement is an adjective → predicate adjective.
If it's a noun → predicate nominative.
"She is happy" (PA). "She is a teacher" (PN).

cards.short: Renames or describes the direct object, not the subject.
cards.detail:
An object complement follows the DO and describes or renames it.

As a noun: "They elected her president." → her = president.
As an adjective: "The news made him anxious." → him = anxious.

…it is the wish of most masters to keep their slaves ignorant. — Douglass. Ignorant completes keep.
OC vs. Subject Complement
Subject complement: describes the subject via a linking verb.
Object complement: describes the DO via an action verb.
"She is happy" (SC). "The joke made her happy" (OC).

cards.short: Some verbs demand a receiver; some are complete alone.
cards.detail:
A transitive verb passes its action to a direct object — the sentence feels unfinished without one (She grabbed —?). An intransitive verb is complete by itself (He slept.).

Many verbs go both ways depending on use: "She sings" (intransitive) / "She sings jazz" (transitive).

Why Writers Care
Knowing what a verb demands tells you what the sentence still owes the reader. "The committee considered" owes us what they considered; "The engine died" owes nothing.

cards.short: Names a person, place, thing, or idea.
cards.detail:
Nouns do the heavy lifting of naming. Every sentence needs at least one — usually as the subject.

Proper: specific name (Yukon, Tuckahoe, Dickinson).
Common: general category (trail, man, house).
Concrete: physical, perceivable (pebbles, carriage, dust).
Abstract: ideas, feelings (truth, knowledge, civility, haste).

Craft Note
Strong writers choose specific nouns over generic ones. Sparrow beats bird; pall beats cloud. Notice how Hemingway chooses channels over streams, and how London reaches for pall — a funeral cloth — instead of gloominess. Specificity is credibility.

cards.short: Stands in for a noun so we aren't endlessly repeating it.
cards.detail:
Pronouns keep language efficient — and they carry enormous weight in literary prose.

Personal: I, you, he, she, it, we, they (+ me, him, her, us, them)
Possessive: my, your, his, her, its, our, their, theirs
Reflexive: myself, yourself, herself, ourselves…
Relative: who, whom, whose, which, that
Demonstrative: this, that, these, those
Indefinite: anyone, everyone, nobody, some, all, each

Craft Note
Pronoun choice signals perspective and intimacy. Hemingway's we lived submerges the individual into a group. Dickinson's He for Death turns an abstraction into a person. Douglass opens his book with I — the grammar of personhood itself. Pronouns are never neutral.

cards.short: Shows action or state of being — the engine of every sentence.
cards.detail:
Every sentence needs a verb. It tells us what the subject does or is.

Action: went, turned, raised, powdered, stopped
Linking: be, seem, become — connect subject to description
Helping / auxiliary: have, be, do, will — combine with a main verb
Modal: must, may, can, could, would — add necessity, possibility, permission

Craft Note
Compare the verbs across the passages: Hemingway's troops went (not marched, advanced); London's day broke (not dawned); Austen's man must be in want (not probably is). Plain verbs feel inevitable; heightened verbs feel stylized. Neither is wrong — but the choice is always meaningful.

cards.short: Describes or modifies a noun or pronoun. Includes the articles a, an, the.
cards.detail:
Adjectives answer: which? what kind? how many? whose?

Descriptive: cold, grey, dry, white, late, rightful
Articles — the most common adjectives of all: a, an, the. The (definite) points to a specific, known noun: the man, the trail. A / an (indefinite) means any member of a class: a truth, a house, a wife — use an before vowel sounds (an hour).
Numerical: nine, twelve, three
Demonstrative: that year, such a man, this truth
Participial: verb-forms doing adjective work — acknowledged, surrounding, little-travelled

Craft Note
London repeats cold and grey four times in one sentence — insistence as style. And the tiny articles carry real weight: Austen writes a truth, not the truth — one letter of irony. London writes the man, not a man — the definite article makes him archetypal. When you write a or the, ask which you mean, and why.

cards.short: Modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb.
cards.detail:
Adverbs answer: when? where? how? to what extent?

• Modifying a verb: stopped kindly, drove slowly
• Modifying an adjective: exceedingly cold
• Modifying another adverb: almost never

Craft Note
Hemingway uses almost no adverbs — swiftly appears once and stops you because it's so rare. London uses exceedingly with deliberate pomposity. Dickinson's kindly makes Death civil. An adverb is a writer leaning in to adjust your reading — notice when they bother.

cards.short: Shows a relationship — usually of space, time, or direction.
cards.detail:
A preposition connects a noun or pronoun to the rest of the sentence.

Common prepositions: in, on, at, by, with, of, for, to, from, about, across, down, near, over, through, like

A preposition always has an object. Together they form a prepositional phrase:
across the river · in the late summer · for Death

Craft Note
Hemingway opens his novel inside a prepositional phrase (In the late summer of that year) — where before who. Douglass stacks five place phrases in his first sentence, building a geographic case for his own existence. Prepositions aren't decoration; they're architecture.

cards.short: Joins words, phrases, or clauses together.
cards.detail:
Coordinating (FANBOYS): for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so — join equal elements.
Subordinating: because, when, although, if, since, while, until, after, before, though — introduce dependent clauses.
Correlative: either/or, neither/nor, not only/but also

Craft Note
Dickinson opens an entire poem with Because — a subordinating conjunction that promises an explanation before delivering anything. Hemingway chains clauses with and… and… and, refusing to rank his facts. The conjunction you choose carries a whole stance toward the reader.

cards.short: Expresses sudden emotion. Grammatically separate.
cards.detail:
Interjections are outbursts — they carry emotion but don't connect to the grammar of the sentence around them.

Examples: Oh! Wow! Alas. Hey. Ouch. Well. Hmm.

They're usually set off by commas or exclamation points:
Oh, I forgot my keys. · Alas! The season is ended.

Craft Note
None of the literary passages here use interjections — which tells you something. Interjections belong to dialogue, informal prose, and heightened emotion. Their absence from formal literary prose is itself a stylistic choice; the register an interjection invokes is everything.

cards.short: The three most common adjectives: a, an, the.
cards.detail: Articles are classified as adjectives — the most frequent ones in English. The (definite) points to a specific, known noun; a / an (indefinite) means any member of a class. Use a before consonant sounds and an before vowel sounds (an hour — the h is silent). See the full Adjective card in the Word Toolbox.

cards.short: Adjectives, adverbs, and phrases that add optional detail.
cards.detail:
Modifiers answer: when? where? how? why? which one? how many?

Adjectives modify nouns: "the red car."
Adverbs modify verbs, adjectives, or adverbs: "ran quickly."
Phrases: "the house on the hill."

Quick Test
Remove the modifier. Still grammatical? Then it was a modifier. "She ran quickly." → "She ran." ✓

cards.short: A structural word that holds the subject slot so the real subject can come later.
cards.detail: In "There were pebbles and boulders" and "It is a truth universally acknowledged," the words there and it refer to nothing — they hold the subject seat so the true subject can arrive after the verb. Writers use the move to control where emphasis lands.

cards.short: A word group that works as a unit — with NO subject-verb pair.
cards.detail:
A phrase is any cluster of words acting together as a single grammatical unit, but lacking the subject + verb pair that would make it a clause.

Every phrase has a "head word" that determines its type:
• preposition → prepositional phrase
• participle → participial phrase
• gerund → gerund phrase
• infinitive marker to + verb → infinitive phrase
• noun renaming a neighbor → appositive phrase

Quick Test
Look for a subject and a finite verb (one that shows tense). If either is missing, you have a phrase — not a clause.

cards.short: Preposition + object (a noun). Acts as an adjective or adverb.
cards.detail:
Structure: preposition + (optional modifiers) + noun/pronoun object.

in the morning · across the dusty field · without him
As an adjective — modifies a noun:
The girl with the red hat waved. (which girl?)
As an adverb — modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb:
She ran into the hallway. (ran where?)
Heads Up
Prepositional phrases can stack: "the man in the house on the corner of Main Street." Each one modifies the noun before it — Douglass stacks five in a single sentence to pin down his birthplace.

cards.short: A noun phrase that renames another noun.
cards.detail:
An appositive sits right next to a noun and provides another name for it — more identification, a title, a definition.

Non-essential (use commas): the info is extra; the sentence works without it.
Mr. Ellis, our principal, gave a speech.
Essential (no commas): the info is needed to identify which one.
The poet Robert Frost wrote about New England. (which poet?)
Comma Choice
Ask: if I removed the appositive, would it still be clear who or what is meant? Clear → commas (the phrase is a bonus). Not clear → no commas (the phrase is doing identification work).

cards.short: Noun + participle. Modifies the whole sentence.
cards.detail:
An absolute phrase is a mini-description — a noun with a participle attached — that hovers over the whole sentence.

Hands trembling, she opened the letter.
The game over, the players shook hands. (noun + understood participle being)
Why use them? Absolutes let you add a detail shot to a whole scene without starting a new clause — a favorite of narrative writers.

Spot the Difference
Absolute: The sun having set, we lit the fire. (has its own noun, sun)
Participial: Having set, the sun painted the sky red. (describes the sentence's subject)

cards.short: Every phrase takes its name from the word that drives it.
cards.detail:
To classify any phrase, find its head word — the word that drives the whole group.

• Starts with a preposition → prepositional
• Starts with a participle (verb form describing a noun) → participial
• Starts with a gerund (-ing form acting as a noun) → gerund
• Starts with to + verb → infinitive
• A noun renaming another noun → appositive
• Noun + participle, modifying the whole sentence → absolute

Double Check
After you spot the head word, ask: "What job is the whole phrase doing?" That's its function (noun, adjective, adverb). Every phrase has both a type AND a function.

cards.short: A verb form doing another part of speech's job.
cards.detail:
A verbal is a word made from a verb that doesn't function as the sentence's verb. Instead it serves as a noun, adjective, or adverb — packing verb energy into another slot.

The three types:
• Participles → act as adjectives
• Gerunds → act as nouns
• Infinitives → act as nouns, adjectives, or adverbs

Why They Matter
The crying baby has more force than the sad baby — the verbal keeps its motion. Verbals are how writers compress action into description, which is why this lesson is called Compressing with Verbals.

cards.short: A verb form acting as an adjective.
cards.detail:
A participle modifies a noun or pronoun — it does an adjective's job. There are two kinds:

Present participle — always ends in -ing:
the crying child · the roaring fire
Past participle — usually ends in -d or -ed, but is often irregular (broken, sung, written, seen, torn, gone):
the shattered window · a worn shoe · a broken promise
Participle vs. Main Verb
"The crying baby" — participle (adjective job).
"The baby is crying" — main verb (present progressive).
A helping verb next to the form usually means it's the sentence's verb; standing alone next to a noun, it's a participle.

cards.short: An -ing verb form acting as a noun.
cards.detail:
A gerund looks exactly like a present participle — same -ing form — but it does a noun's work: naming an activity.

As subject:
Swimming is great exercise.
As direct object:
She loves painting.
As predicate nominative:
His passion is teaching.
As object of a preposition:
…excusing the act to himself by looking at his watch. — London
Gerund vs. Participle
Ask: is the -ing word NAMING something (gerund) or DESCRIBING a noun (participle)?
"Running is fun" → names it → gerund.
"The running water" → describes water → participle.

cards.short: 'To' + base verb. Acts as noun, adjective, or adverb.
cards.detail:
The most versatile verbal: to + the base form of a verb.

As a noun:
To forgive is human. (subject)
She wants to leave. (direct object)
As an adjective (modifies a noun):
a reason to celebrate · homework to finish
As an adverb (usually purpose):
She trained hard to win. (why did she train?)
Don't Confuse
"to + noun" is a PREPOSITIONAL phrase (to the store).
"to + verb" is an infinitive (to study).
The word after to tells you which.

cards.short: A participle plus its objects and modifiers.
cards.detail:
When a participle brings along other words, you have a participial phrase. The whole phrase acts as an adjective.

Frozen solid from the overnight storm, the pond glittered.
…he paused for breath at the top, excusing the act to himself… — London
Keep It Attached
A participial phrase at the start of a sentence describes the subject that follows — so make sure they match. "Running down the street, my hat flew off" accidentally puts the hat in sneakers; "Running down the street, I lost my hat" says what happened. Readers will follow the grammar, not your intention.

cards.short: A gerund plus its objects and modifiers.
cards.detail:
A gerund phrase behaves like a single noun — you can swap in "it" to confirm.

Eating leafy greens daily improves digestion. (subject)
We celebrated by ordering pizza. (object of preposition)
A Register Choice
Before a gerund, formal written English prefers the possessive: "I appreciate your helping me" (like "I appreciate your help"). In conversation, "I appreciate you helping me" is everywhere. Match the formality of the occasion — that's a choice, not a trap.

cards.short: An infinitive plus its objects and modifiers.
cards.detail:
Like the infinitive alone, an infinitive phrase can function as noun, adjective, or adverb — determined by its job.

To become a doctor requires years of study. (noun — subject)
He bought a book to read on the plane. (adjective — modifies book)
She whispered to avoid waking the baby. (adverb — modifies whispered)
Split Infinitives
"To boldly go where no one has gone before" — the adverb sits inside the infinitive. Once considered taboo, now fine. Clarity beats rules; split it if it reads better.

cards.short: Form can fool you. Function is the only reliable test.
cards.detail:
Because verbals share their forms with main verbs, you can't identify them by spelling alone. Always ask: what job is this word doing in its sentence?

Three checks:
1. Is there a helping verb nearby? (is, was, has been, will be…) → probably part of the main verb.
2. What question does the word answer? Which / what kind → adjective (participle). What / it → noun (gerund, or infinitive-as-noun). When / why / how → adverb (infinitive).
3. What's the sentence's main verb? Find that first; everything else must be doing another job.

The Payoff
Once you can spot verbals reliably, whole sentences open up — you'll see writers compress a paragraph of action into one participial phrase. Grammar becomes craft.

cards.short: A word group WITH a subject + verb pair.
cards.detail:
A clause contains both a subject and a finite verb (a verb that takes a tense, like runs, ran, is running).

Every clause falls into one of two groups:
• Independent — can stand alone as a sentence.
• Dependent — cannot stand alone; it attaches to an independent clause.

The S+V Test
Point to the subject. Point to the verb. Both there? It's a clause, not a phrase. That single test decides everything.

cards.short: S + V + complete thought. Stands on its own.
cards.detail:
An independent clause has everything a sentence needs: subject, verb, complete thought.

The storm passed.
My brother makes the best pancakes.
Every sentence contains at least one. Compound and compound-complex sentences contain two or more.

Joining Independent Clauses
Three standard welds:
1. Comma + FANBOYS (for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so)
2. A semicolon
3. A semicolon + conjunctive adverb (however, therefore)
A comma alone (a comma splice) reads to most audiences as two sentences leaning on a hinge that isn't there — save it for deliberate, informal effect.

cards.short: Has S+V, but starts with a subordinator — can't stand alone.
cards.detail:
A dependent clause has a subject and verb, but one word at the front makes it lean on the rest of the sentence:

• Subordinating conjunctions: because, when, although, if, since, while, until, after, before, though, unless, so that…
• Relative pronouns: who, whom, whose, which, that
• Interrogative / "wh-" words: what, whatever, whoever, how, why, whether

Because I was late, I missed the bus.
Alone, "Because I was late" is a promise with no payoff — the reader waits for the rest.

Three Jobs
Every dependent clause acts as a noun, an adjective, or an adverb. That job determines its type.

cards.short: A dependent clause acting as a noun.
cards.detail:
A noun clause fills a noun slot — anywhere a single noun could sit.

Common starters: that, what, whatever, who, whoever, whether, if, why, how, when, where

As subject:
That she won shocked no one.
As direct object:
I know what you did last summer.
As predicate nominative:
The problem is that we're out of time.
As object of preposition:
Give the prize to whoever finishes first.
The "It" Test
Replace the whole clause with "it." If the sentence still works, it's a noun clause.

cards.short: A dependent clause acting as an adjective — modifies a noun.
cards.detail:
Also called a relative clause. It sits right after the noun it describes and usually starts with a relative pronoun: who, whom, whose, which, that.

The scientist who discovered penicillin was Alexander Fleming.
Restrictive (essential, no commas): identifies which one.
Students who study daily succeed.
Non-restrictive (extra info, commas): adds detail to an already-identified noun.
My sister, who lives in Boston, is visiting.
That vs. Which
Standard American usage: that for restrictive clauses (no commas), which for non-restrictive (with commas). Who/whom works for both; the comma choice still signals essential vs. extra.

cards.short: A dependent clause acting as an adverb — modifies a verb, adjective, or adverb.
cards.detail:
An adverb clause starts with a subordinating conjunction and answers an adverb question: when? where? why? how? under what condition?

By meaning:
• Time: when, before, after, until, since, while, as
• Cause: because, since, as
• Purpose: so that, in order that
• Condition: if, unless, provided that
• Concession: although, though, even though, while
• Comparison: than, as…as

When the movie ended, we left. (time)
He succeeded because he worked hard. (reason)
Comma Pattern
Adverb clause first → comma after it. Adverb clause last → usually no comma. When he called, I answered. / I answered when he called.

cards.short: One independent clause.
cards.detail:
A simple sentence contains exactly one independent clause and no dependent clauses. It may still have phrases and multiple subjects or verbs.

Birds fly.
The old oak tree in our yard fell during the storm. (still simple — one S+V)
Maya and Jonas studied and aced the exam. (compound subject + compound verb, still ONE clause)
Easy to Misread
Length doesn't matter. A sentence with twelve prepositional phrases can still be "simple" if it has one S+V pair and no dependent clauses.

cards.short: Two or more independent clauses joined together.
cards.detail:
A compound sentence joins two (or more) independent clauses as equals. Three standard joins:

1. Comma + FANBOYS:
I wanted to go, but I was too tired.
2. Semicolon (clauses closely related):
The rain stopped; we went outside.
3. Semicolon + conjunctive adverb:
She studied hard; however, the test covered other units.
What Coordination Says
Joining with and or but presents two ideas as equal partners. If one idea should lean on the other, subordinate instead — that's a complex sentence.

cards.short: One independent + one or more dependent clauses.
cards.detail:
A complex sentence has exactly one independent clause and at least one dependent clause.

When the alarm rang, I woke up.
The book that I'm reading is fascinating.
Why Subordinate?
Complex sentences show the relationship between ideas — cause (because), time (when), contrast (although), condition (if) — and they decide which idea is the headline and which is background. Same facts, different emphasis: that's the writer's call.

cards.short: At least two independents AND at least one dependent.
cards.detail:
The heavyweight: at least two independent clauses AND at least one dependent clause.

Although it was cold, we went outside, and we built a snowman.
Dependent: Although it was cold
Independent 1: we went outside
Independent 2: we built a snowman

Variety Is the Point
Good writing mixes all four sentence types. Too many simple sentences feel choppy; too many compound-complex feel bloated. Rhythm comes from variation — that's Lesson 07.

cards.short: Where a sentence starts controls what a reader expects.
cards.detail:
Most English sentences open with the subject — which is exactly why an occasional different opener wakes the reader up.

Prepositional phrase: In the late summer of that year, we lived…
Participial phrase: Exhausted from the climb, the hikers rested.
Adverb clause: Because I could not stop for Death, He kindly stopped for me.
Adverb: Slowly, the carriage rolled on.

The Ratio
Openers are seasoning. If every sentence opens with a flourish, the flourish disappears. Vary a few sentences per paragraph and let the rest run subject-first.

cards.short: Long sentences build; short sentences land.
cards.detail:
Readers hear prose. A string of same-length sentences drones; variation makes music.

London writes a long, winding sentence about the trail and the timberland — then: It was nine o'clock. Four words. The short sentence lands like a stamp because the long one came first.

Revision Move
Read your paragraph aloud. If every sentence takes the same breath, combine two of them (Lessons 04–06 give you the tools) and cut one down to under six words.

cards.short: The end of a sentence is its loudest position.
cards.detail:
Readers remember best what comes last. Austen builds an entire philosophical windup just to land on a wife; Douglass ends his paragraph on ignorant.

Moves that control emphasis:
• Delay the subject with an expletive (It is a truth…)
• Subordinate the background so the headline stands alone (Lesson 06)
• Reorder: "She won the championship after years of setbacks" vs. "After years of setbacks, she won the championship."

Ask of Every Draft Sentence
What word does this sentence end on? Is that the word you want ringing in the reader's ear?
